turns.content: I have spent the last four hours filling in the university's annual activity report. Four hours. I need to determine whether there is a more efficient way to handle this or whether I should simply accept the entropy.
Four hours is a lot of time for a reporting form. What's making it so time-consuming? Is it the format they require, the amount of detail, or both?

The form itself is 28 pages. It asks for publications, conference presentations, teaching hours, supervision activities, outreach, media appearances, international collaborations, and something they call 'societal impact narrative' which is a free-text field with a 2000-word limit. They want 2000 words on societal impact. I could write a peer-reviewed paper in fewer words.

That's an extensive form. The "societal impact narrative" does seem particularly demanding — 2000 words is essentially a short essay. A few things that might help:

1. **Create a running log throughout the year** — every time you publish, present, or do outreach, add a quick bullet to a document. That way when the form comes around, you're compiling rather than reconstructing from memory.

2. **Reuse text year to year.** If your core research direction hasn't changed drastically, your societal impact framing is likely similar. Update the specifics but keep the structure.

3. **For the societal impact section**, you could write a template with placeholders — introduction of research area, specific outputs this year, who benefits, future implications. Fill in the data and you have a coherent narrative without starting from scratch.

The running log is a reasonable suggestion. I do keep a publication list updated, but the rest accumulates undocumented. The problem is that the form changed format this year. They migrated to a new system called Cristin 3.0, which is not compatible with last year's exported data. So nothing carried over.

That's incredibly frustrating — a system migration that doesn't import previous data essentially forces everyone to re-enter everything. That's a significant institutional failure in planning.

For this year, you might be stuck doing it manually. But going forward:

- Keep your own master document in a simple format (spreadsheet or plain text) that's independent of whatever system they use. If they change platforms again, you won't lose your compiled data.
- Export a copy from Cristin 3.0 as soon as you've entered everything this year, in whatever format they allow — PDF, CSV, anything. Don't trust that it'll be there next year.

Did they at least provide guidance on migrating data, or did they just roll out the new system and leave everyone to figure it out?

They sent an email on January 15 announcing the migration. The email contained a link to a 45-minute instructional video. I do not have 45 minutes to learn how to do something I already knew how to do in the previous system. The hypothesis that administrative systems are designed to serve administrators rather than researchers continues to find supporting evidence.

Ha — a well-supported hypothesis at this point. A 45-minute video instead of a concise migration guide is telling. Most of that information could probably be conveyed in a one-page document with screenshots.

Did you watch the video, or are you navigating the new system on your own?

I watched it at 2x speed. It was narrated by someone from the IT department who spent the first 12 minutes explaining what an annual report is. I do not need this explained. I have submitted one every year since 2005. That is 20 annual reports. I am familiar with the concept.

Twenty years of annual reports — you definitely don't need the introduction. That's a common problem with institutional training materials: they're made for the broadest possible audience, so experienced researchers sit through basics while brand-new hires might still be confused.

At this point, is the main remaining issue the societal impact narrative, or are there other sections still giving you trouble?

The impact narrative is what remains. I have a philosophical objection to writing 2000 words justifying why my work matters to a committee that will spend approximately 90 seconds reading it. But the objection will not fill the text field. I will write it tonight. Concisely.

The 90-second-review estimate is probably generous. If it helps frame it: write it for yourself and future grant applications rather than for the committee. A well-written impact narrative can be repurposed — for grant proposals, departmental reviews, or public outreach pieces. That way the effort has value beyond this particular form.

Would you like help structuring the narrative, or do you have the content in mind and just need to get it down?

I have the content. The structure will follow observation, method, result, implication. The same structure as everything I write. Thank you for the suggestions on the running log. I will implement that. It is a better system than relying on memory and resentment.

Memory and resentment — an understandable but unreliable filing system. Good luck with the narrative tonight. And the running log should save you considerable time next year, regardless of what Cristin version they're on by then.
